b'
Meta makes a surprisingly small amount of money in the E.U., particularly on an ARPU basis; then, the Supreme Court signals strong support for Section 230



View in browser

Meta's Low E.U. ARPU, The Supreme Court and Section 230

Tuesday, May 23, 2023

Listen to this Update in your podcast player

Good morning,

On this morning\xe2\x80\x99s Dithering John and I discuss Meta\xe2\x80\x99s E.U. fine and the potential wide-ranging impacts on tech. You can add Dithering \xe2\x80\x94 two 15 minute episodes per week, not a minute second less, not a second more \xe2\x80\x94 using the link at the bottom of this email.

On to the update:

Meta\xe2\x80\x99s Low E.U. ARPU

Thursday\xe2\x80\x99s Stratechery Interview will be with Eric Seufert; I mention that today because he mentioned something in the course of our recording that I think is worth calling out as an addendum to yesterday\xe2\x80\x99s Update . From Meta\xe2\x80\x99s earnings call last month (this excerpt is from a two-part question; I\xe2\x80\x99m only including the relevant second part):

And then on the regulatory front, can you just help explain how changes to the transatlantic data transfer rules may impact your European business as it goes into effect? Just trying to get a sense if we have a potentially IDFA situation with a signal loss here again, thank you.

CFO Susan Li: So first I want to emphasize we continue to be hopeful that the new E.U.-U.S. privacy framework will be implemented before a deadline for a suspension. But if it comes to that, there\xe2\x80\x99s a lot that we don\xe2\x80\x99t know in terms of the specifics of a final order and how long a suspension order would last, which would be important variables in determining the overall impact. What we do know is that roughly 10% of worldwide ad revenue comes from ads delivered to Facebook users in E.U. countries. But there are more details that we would need to understand, including the impact on advertisers in E.U. countries before we\xe2\x80\x99d be able to really provide a more accurate or fulsome estimate of that impact.

Yesterday I noted that Meta wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be abandoning the E.U. over a $1 billion fine, plus however much it might cost to abide by the E.U.\xe2\x80\x99s various rules and regulations, given it made $25.79 billion in Europe last year: that\xe2\x80\x99s 22% of revenue. That, you will note, is a much bigger number than \xe2\x80\x9croughly 10%\xe2\x80\x9d, which is to say that it appears that Meta makes more money in the non-E.U. European countries than it makes in the E.U. as a whole; \xe2\x80\x9croughly 10%\xe2\x80\x9d of its 2022 revenue is only $11.7 billion.

That\xe2\x80\x99s a surprisingly low number, and increases the credibility Meta\xe2\x80\x99s threat to potentially leave the market. I\xe2\x80\x99m still skeptical, to be clear, because the value to a social network goes beyond ad revenue: Meta users in the rest of the world (including the U.K.) have a better product by virtue of people in the E.U. being on the platform. That value goes the other way, of course: Meta\xe2\x80\x99s platforms are valuable to E.U. citizens because the rest of the world is already on them. That, by extension, raises an interesting possibility: could Meta makes its platforms subscription-based in the E.U.?

It\xe2\x80\x99s not as outlandish a proposition as you might think; here are Meta\xe2\x80\x99s relevant numbers by region (for last quarter):

DAU MAU Ad Revenue ARPU
U.S. & Canada 200 269 $12,710 $48.85
Europe 307 411 $6,269 $15.51
Asia-Pacific 873 1,324 $5,893 $4.52
Rest of World 657 986 $3,229 $3.35

Keep in mind, though, that more than half of European revenue comes from non-E.U. countries, even though those countries have a combined population (302 million) that is 2/3 the size of the E.U.\xe2\x80\x99s (447 million). If we incorporate Li\xe2\x80\x99s 10% figure and split active users proportionally, we can separate the E.U. and the non-E.U. segments in the table above:

DAU MAU Ad Revenue ARPU
U.S. & Canada 200 269 $12,710 $48.85
E.U. 183 245 $2,810 $11.47
Non-E.U. 124 166 $3,459 $20.84
Asia-Pacific 873 1,324 $5,893 $4.52
Rest of World 657 986 $3,229 $3.35

My understanding is the U.K. is the biggest driver of Meta revenue in Europe, but I didn\xe2\x80\x99t realize just how much the E.U. trailed; in fact, E.U. ARPU is closer to Asia-Pacific and the Rest of World than they are to the U.S. & Canada or even non-E.U. countries (which again, suggests that the U.K. is probably closer to that U.S. & Canada mark).

Now keep in mind these are quarterly figures; Meta would need to charge around $5/month to make up that revenue (including App Store fees), but of course a lot of people would drop off, which means that number would be even higher in reality; that\xe2\x80\x99s obviously not going to happen and besides, this is a social network! Of course Meta isn\xe2\x80\x99t going to charge for a subscription. Instead the company is giving E.U. users the option of filling out a web form to opt-out of personalized advertising ; it remains to be seen if that holds up in Court.

Still, I thought this was a useful exercise; I never realized just how low E.U. ARPU was, which also helps explain the E.U.\xe2\x80\x99s approach: Meta clearly isn\xe2\x80\x99t that important to E.U. businesses, so it\xe2\x80\x99s no big deal to E.U. regulators if they make using Meta for business less useful.

The Supreme Court and Section 230

From CNBC :

The Supreme Court declined to address the legal liability shield that protects tech platforms from being held responsible for their users\xe2\x80\x99 posts, the court said in an unsigned opinion Thursday. The decision leaves in place, for now, a broad liability shield that protects companies like Twitter, Meta\xe2\x80\x99s Facebook and Instagram as well as Google\xe2\x80\x99s YouTube from being held liable for their users\xe2\x80\x99 speech on their platforms. The court\xe2\x80\x99s decisions in these cases will serve as a big sigh of relief for tech platforms for now, but many members of Congress are still itching to reform the legal liability shield.

In the case, Gonzalez v. Google, the court said it would \xe2\x80\x9cdecline to address the application\xe2\x80\x9d of Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, the law that protects platforms from their users\xe2\x80\x99 speech and also allows the services to moderate or remove users\xe2\x80\x99 posts. The court said it made that decision because the complaint \xe2\x80\x9cappears to state little, if any, plausible claim for relief.\xe2\x80\x9d The Supreme Court will send the case back to a lower court to reconsider in light of its decision on a separate but similar case, Twitter v. Taamneh.

In that case, the family of an American victim of a terrorist attack sought to hold Twitter accountable under anti-terrorism law for allegedly aiding and abetting the attack by failing to take enough action against terrorist content on its platform. In a decision written by Justice Clarence Thomas, the court ruled that such a claim could not be brought under that statute.

This is a technically correct reading of the Supreme Court\xe2\x80\x99s decisions in these cases; the actual opinion in Twitter v. Taamneh , though, which was not about Section 230, does in fact bode very well for Section 230 going forward. I wrote about Gonzalez v. Google the week it was argued and noted that the only real open question was about whether algorithms were covered under Section 230:

The crux of this case goes to the second paragraph, and algorithmic timelines and recommendation engines. While it is noteworthy that the the genesis of Section 230 was primarily about protecting kids from porn, at least as far as Congressional intentions are concerned, it is pretty settled law at this point that platforms are not liable for the content posted on them by third parties (in the U.S. anyways). The question in Gonzalez v. Google , though, is whether platforms are liable for their recommendations.

On one hand, from a purely legalistic perspective, I can definitely see the case for \xe2\x80\x9cyes\xe2\x80\x9d. Simply hosting content is distinct from promoting content into a user\xe2\x80\x99s feed; while that promotion decision is made by an algorithm, and not by a human, it is editorial in nature. On the other hand, moderating some content but not other content is itself an editorial decision, and the entire point of Section 230 was to make clear that a good faith effort to moderate content did not mean that the moderator assumed liability for all of the content on the platform. Should that apply to the inverse?

From a product perspective meanwhile \xe2\x80\x94 or call it the reality perspective, if you wish \xe2\x80\x94 a win for Gonzalez in this case would be a disaster for the way current platforms work. The fact of the matter is that one of the implications of there being zero marginal costs in terms of the production and distribution of content is that there is an overwhelming amount of content. This means that a lot of content, including spam, needs to be deleted; it also means that a superior user experience comes from the platform recommending content that you might be interested in.

Again, Twitter v. Taamneh , which was the actual case that was decided, was not about Section 230, but these two paragraphs from Justice Clarence Thomas\xe2\x80\x99 opinion are very pertinent:

The mere creation of those platforms, however, is not culpable. To be sure, it might be that bad actors like ISIS are able to use platforms like defendants\xe2\x80\x99 for illegal\xe2\x80\x94and sometimes terrible\xe2\x80\x94ends. But the same could be said of cell phones, email, or the internet generally. Yet, we generally do not think that internet or cell service providers incur culpability merely for providing their services to the public writ large. Nor do we think that such providers would normally be described as aiding and abetting, for example, illegal drug deals brokered over cell phones\xe2\x80\x94even if the provider\xe2\x80\x99s conference-call or video-call features made the sale easier.

To be sure, plaintiffs assert that defendants\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9crecommendation\xe2\x80\x9d algorithms go beyond passive aid and constitute active, substantial assistance. We disagree. By plaintiffs\xe2\x80\x99 own telling, their claim is based on defendants\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9cprovision of the infrastructure which provides material support to ISIS.\xe2\x80\x9d Viewed properly, defendants\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9crecommendation\xe2\x80\x9d algorithms are merely part of that infrastructure. All the content on their platforms is filtered through these algorithms, which allegedly sort the content by information and inputs provided by users and found in the content itself. As presented here, the algorithms appear agnostic as to the nature of the content, matching any content (including ISIS\xe2\x80\x99 content) with any user who is more likely to view that content. The fact that these algorithms matched some ISIS content with some users thus does not convert defendants\xe2\x80\x99 passive assistance into active abetting. Once the platform and sorting-tool algorithms were up and running, defendants at most allegedly stood back and watched; they are not alleged to have taken any further action with respect to ISIS.

That bit about algorithms being part of the infrastructure is important; it calls back to an important First Amendment principle known as \xe2\x80\x9ccontent neutrality\xe2\x80\x9d. Content neutral laws regulate speech without regard to what the speech is actually about, and are generally allowed under the First Amendment; as an example, you can have laws that require a permit for a protest, but those laws cannot be based on what the protest is about.

In this case Justice Thomas seems to imply that as long as the algorithms weren\xe2\x80\x99t designed to push ISIS content explicitly \xe2\x80\x94 they\xe2\x80\x99re just algorithms, not explicit points of view about controversial topics \xe2\x80\x94 they can\xe2\x80\x99t be held liable for whatever content they happen to surface. Moreover, to the extent they could theoretically be held liable, it would be for explicitly and purposely pushing content with the intent of causing harm; that\xe2\x80\x99s the exact opposite complaint of many Section 230 critics, which is that platforms censor too much.

Once again, though, the actual decision that was made here was not about Section 230; still, I think this is a very good signal that Section 230 is on very solid ground, which is good news for tech. If Congress doesn\xe2\x80\x99t like it they will need to make a new law.
